chapters.title: Humor as a job
chapters.text:
There were times when Eric Thomas had two jobs that kept him busy. By day, he occupied a program director position at an LGBTQ community center and moonlighted by posting funny and sarcastic comments about politics for ELLE.com. He had to wake up quite early to search for any political news he could talk about on ELLE.com, try to write a decent text that same morning, and then rush to the center.

Thomas wasn't an Internet-y person; he wasn't that into surfing for memes or news. But he was quite successful in being hilarious and reposted by numerous people on Facebook. ELLE.com noticed him when he gained relative popularity by commenting on politics on this social media platform. However, the author wittily noted that his illusions of fame quickly faded. Unless you contribute 24/7, the Internet will forget you the next day, even if your excellent post gets lots of likes and reposts.

He thinks that writing is an art and requires love and commitment. Doing art just for money wouldn't work out but receiving paychecks for your creativity is quite fulfilling. However, when your work is on display all the time, you start questioning who your audience is and their intentions. Writing a column for ELLE.com was a confusing experience. He realized he could only win if he were the first to post funny political jokes and comments. So, he stayed up late or woke up in the middle of the night to write a funny joke.

Thomas pursues his hilariousness as a source of pure motives because he can give his readers hope.

Humor is a perfect savior when you think that you have failed everything.

This summary will acquaint you with Eric Thomas’s struggles. He has come a long way from a young man who did not understand himself to a man who is happy professionally and personally, so you won’t feel alone.

chapters.title: Sometimes life is unfair, but you have to keep fighting
chapters.text:
The environment in which you grow up matters to your future. Although Eric Thomas wasn't born into a wealthy family, his parents did everything to give him and his brothers a promising future, working from morning until night and not buying themselves new clothes for years to pay for their son's education. They did not give up despite all the difficulties of living in an area where drug trafficking flourished and turned to officials and city institutions to improve conditions. Sometimes their cravings were answered, and sometimes they were not, but they never stopped trying to create a better life for themselves and their children. Of course, their sons adopted their behavior model. At age six, Eric Thomas wrote a letter to the TV channel demanding they rebroadcast his favorite film. Although the company never returned the movie to TV channels, they sent several gifts in response to the letter. Ultimately, Thomas realized he had written a complaint letter, not for nothing.

Life doesn't always turn out how you want, but you can do something about it.

As a child, the future writer understood what financial restrictions were. He knew that products should be bought strictly according to the list and not according to desire or whim. Of course, Thomas did not like that stupid list, but today he is thankful for the lessons learned in childhood. He admires his parents, who chose their children's future and denied themselves. Thomas' parents taught him to control his budget, which he still does today. He perfectly understands what a great gift his parents gave him — they did not have enough money but found a way to educate their sons and create a separate world for their children, without sorrow and sadness. Perhaps it was then that Thomas realized the value of humor in a complex and unpredictable life. Later, he still had to go beyond the bubble created by his parents who taught him to fight for his happiness and face the reality that he still did not understand who he was.

chapters.title: Feeling different emotions at once is okay
chapters.text:
In his school years, Eric Thomas first faced the problem of self-identification and self-understanding. He didn't know he was romantically attracted to the same sex in high school. He had crushes on guys but tried not to determine whether he thought these guys were cool or he was falling in love. Thomas had infatuations with both sexes in life, books, and movies, but admitting that he might have been gay was pretty dangerous during the 90s. So, he decided to think of himself as a cheerful person with a loving nature.

A new African American girl named Electra showed up at his school. She seemed to be keeping aloof from her peers, but Thomas remembered their first conversation in the library. Electra told him that she had moved to his city, Baltimore, from New York and that her mother had died. She was surprisingly honest about her vulnerability, especially for a teenager. He saw grief in her eyes, but it didn't impede her from having fun and moving on with her life.

He was a member of the Black Awareness Club at the school and was always interested in why this new girl never joined them. Electra was trying to be more sociable and make new friends, so, wasn't it just natural for her to join? So, months after their talks and chats, he finally dared to ask, and she answered with her usual calm straightforwardness that she simply didn't want to join. Thomas was astonished by her answer, and, like a bolt of lightning, he realized that things could be as simple as that.

It was possible to be authentic and black and aware and not part of a club, much like it was possible to be constantly hounded by grief, yet funny and charming. ~ R. Eric Thomas

A person can easily reconcile different emotions and principles and remain integral. Grieving doesn't mean you can't be happy, excited, or funny. Even though you can believe in something wholeheartedly, it doesn't mean you have to declare it loud and clear if you are not ready or don't want to.

Life isn't about absolutes. There is no black, white, or grey to it. The true art of living is about finding your unique way to blend life's different contrasts.

chapters.title: Being courageous will help you become happier
chapters.text:
The next stage of self-discovery began when Thomas entered college. One of the orientation week requirements included diversity meetings. At these discussions, the facilitators taught students to use neutral, non-offensive language towards people of color and members of the LGBTQ community. But in the 90s, matters of the LGBTQ community were still sensitive to talk about, and one incident showed that even facilitators could make mistakes.

One of the facilitators was queer, and during a regular meeting, an African American boy asked which words were politically correct for LGBTQ people. The facilitator asked him whether the boy was gay, which was unexpected and risky. From then on, moderators used this incident to teach attendants that vigilance in such matters is vital.

Thomas decided to visit a Coming Out Group to make himself more comfortable and get support as an LGBTQ person. However, he misinterpreted the concept — it was a support group, not a secret organization. The boy treated it wrong and even started behaving like a spy. He hid in the bushes near the chapel where the group held their meetings to observe how the members entered and left. These people's diversity and uniqueness amused him, but his fun playing a spy didn't resolve his worries concerning his sexual orientation. While others were getting answers and support, Thomas stood outside the chapel in the dirt, feeling the weight of the world.

Despite your fear of opening up about your problem, some people will support you if you show that you need their help.

The second year of college added another challenge when Thomas received an email from the Black Student Union. They asked him to mentor an African American first-year student named Quentin Brick. He was surprised that he didn't get a mentor during his first year in college, nervous about becoming one, and felt like Quentin was stuck with him for no good. So, Thomas didn't email him since he thought he had nothing to offer, but Quentin took charge and wrote an offer to meet for a little chat. During the meeting, Thomas tried to pretend he was a wise mentor who had many answers and was approachable. In the end, Thomas learned a lot about himself after this meeting. Later, he and Quentin developed a romantic relationship that made Thomas discover how far he still had to go before realizing his identity.

chapters.title: Humor inspired by pain can hurt the author of the joke
chapters.text:
Life in New York during his college years wasn't easy for Thomas. He was probably one of those rare people who came to New York with internal resistance about being gay. Usually, New York embraced all the differences and allowed everybody to express themselves how they wished. The author felt sad all the time and even got used to it. Naturally, he failed his studies because he was in a constant state of frustration. Thomas received a letter saying he was on academic probation, so he returned home feeling like he had failed his parents and his race.

Rosa Parks didn't sit on that bus for me to go to New York and turn gay. ~ R. Eric Thomas

Like Rosa Parks, who didn't give up her seat on the bus because she was tired of oppression, Thomas couldn't stay silent about African Americans. When he worked for a Baltimore paper, he accepted complaints and wrote movie reviews. But one day, the young man decided to change to a more fulfilling career. He saw a sign in a bookstore saying From Bondage to Books: Black History Month with pictures of Colin Powell, the first African American secretary of state, and Harriet Tubman, an African American abolitionist. Thomas suddenly got angry because the sign implied that the only history African Americans could have revolved around slavery. So, he decided to write a satire on this matter.

Unfortunately, he wasn't that good at satire, and people misunderstood him. Thomas named his article An Idiot's Guide to Black History Month. The whole mood of his writing conveyed the idea that Black History Month isn't essential, but he didn't think so in reality.

Be careful when using humor in sensitive topics. It's better to highlight that you are being sarcastic to avoid misunderstanding.

Worth attention, the byline named the author R. Eric Thomas, a race-neutral name, and people thought that a European American guy who was unbelievably racist wrote the article. The text went viral, and soon Thomas started to receive hate mail. His community was biased against him, and he felt left out and lonely again. Thomas began to explore his feelings and realized that that sign in the bookstore had hurt his feelings. Although he wrote a satire, anger boiled inside him, which the readers felt. He received hate mail for a long time until Black History Month ended, but more importantly, is what he learned about his feelings.

Did you know? Middle Passage is a historical term describing the route taken by slave ships from Africa to America.

chapters.title: The secret of storytelling
chapters.text:
In 2011, Thomas performed on an open mic show, and the audience loved him. However, he didn't feel like he shared 100% of his dark thoughts and feelings that he sometimes couldn't even process himself. That’s why Thomas related to the songs of Kanye West, who wrote about suicidal thoughts and the final achievement of triumph. He even got used to his suicidal thoughts and took them as a given; he didn't know what to change to become the person he wanted to be.

As a storyteller loved by the public, Thomas realized he might teach others how to tell stories. During his workshops, he emphasized that a crucial component of a catching story was answering the question, “Why are you telling this story?” You may begin by telling how you entered the subway and end with how you left it, but what happened in the middle, and why are you telling it? Thomas wanted to know why his story was going on. He seemed lost in a world full of stories and opportunities. And then Thomas figured out what he wanted: a career, love (for someone and himself), being loved, and having hope. But how could he tell a new story without experiencing it first? Storytelling helped him realize the need for closeness and love.

And here he is now, a successful comic and writer who has been through a lot and married Reverend David Thomas. His story of marriage shows that, despite all his challenges concerning the self-acceptance of his sexual orientation, he ended up in a loving relationship. He and his future significant other met on a panel about LGBTQ people of faith. He liked David a lot and even wondered how to flirt with him. But Thomas couldn't visit David's church for the time they were dating because it would have been weird to have a relationship with someone from a congregation. The couple figured out all the difficulties and stayed together. What a love story!

It must be some love law: you meet your future spouse in the most unpredictable place and moment.

Eric Thomas has come a long way to accept himself for who he is. His effort and concern for himself made him a happy and self-fulfilled person. It wasn't easy, but he managed it thanks to self-belief and humor.

chapters.title: Conclusion
chapters.text:
Eric Thomas shows that despite all the difficulties in life, everybody can triumph in the end. People make mistakes and wrong choices, but it doesn't mean they will have bad lives. We explore the world daily, and it's okay to be lost occasionally. It feels painful for everyone, but we are stronger than we think.

His approach to life, upbeat personality, and jokes helped him get through the sad days. However hard it was, he achieved success, developed his career, and married a man he loved. He struggled with his identity for a long time since he didn't have the freedom to admit his sexual orientation. But he didn't give up, and, in the end, he found his soulmate. Struggling with sexual identity is hard when society doesn't accept someone for who they are. Thomas' persistence in researching and trying to love himself helped him become a happy person who shares his challenges and joyful moments with the audience.

Thomas's positivity helps to understand that everybody goes through an internal battle daily. People have broken hearts, sadness, and grief. But despite all that, we can feel better, happier, and more satisfied with our lives. We can combine grief and fun because life isn't only black or white; it is a mixture of both, and when we have a little black, we can always look at something white.

Try this

Being more positive about your problems will help you perceive life through a cheerful lens. Let's use Eric Thomas's method here. When an issue occurs, think about how you can make fun of it. Try to push it to absurdity, where the problem will seem less threatening and dispiriting.

Read it till the end?
